name: Yayoi Kusama
artStatement: My art originates from hallucinations only I can see. I translate the obsessive visions and the infinite nets of dots that have flooded my mind since childhood into paintings, sculptures, and environments. Through repetition and accumulation I seek the self-obliteration of the individual into the infinite — a love for the universe that erases the boundary between body and cosmos.

studio.visits: Private working studio; not open to the public. Inquiries via her studio.

headshots.credit: Photo: George Quasha, “art is (Speaking Portraits)” — CC BY 3.0 via Wikimedia Commons
Studio portrait, Tokyo

education: Kyoto Municipal School of Arts and Crafts — Nihonga painting (1948–1949)
Art Students League of New York (1958)
awards: U.S. Department of State Medal of Arts (2012)

interviews.title: Yayoi Kusama: The Universe of Infinite Dots

press.title: How Yayoi Kusama Conquered the Art World
Inside the Mind of the Polka-Dot Queen

books.title: Infinity Net: The Autobiography of Yayoi Kusama
Yayoi Kusama: Every Day I Pray for Love

publicCollections: The Museum of Modern Art, New York
National Museum of Modern Art, Tokyo

series.name: Infinity Nets
series.description: Vast monochrome canvases built from repeated, hand-painted loops — the foundational gesture of accumulation and self-obliteration.

series.name: Infinity Mirror Rooms
series.description: Immersive mirrored environments filled with lights and forms that dissolve the viewer into endlessly reflected space.

works.title: Infinity Mirrored Room — The Souls of Millions of Light Years Away

series.name: Pumpkins
series.description: The artist's beloved motif rendered as paintings and monumental polka-dot sculptures — humble, humorous, and deeply personal.

videos.title: Yayoi Kusama: Infinity Mirror Rooms at Tate Modern

name: El Anatsui
artStatement: I work with media that have a history of human touch — discarded bottle caps, printing plates, milk-tin lids. By stitching thousands of fragments together with copper wire I make works that are deliberately non-fixed: they can be draped, folded, and re-formed at every installation. Form should remain free. My materials carry the memory of trade, consumption, and the meeting of cultures across the Atlantic.

studio.visits: Active working studio; visits by arrangement through his galleries.

headshots.credit: Portrait placeholder — no freely-licensed photo of the artist was available

education: BA Sculpture, University of Science and Technology, Kumasi, Ghana (1968)
Postgraduate Diploma in Art Education, Kumasi (1969)
awards: Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement, Venice Biennale (2015)

press.title: El Anatsui Turns Bottle Caps Into Gold
The Sculptor Who Drapes Buildings in Metal

publicCollections: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
Smithsonian National Museum of African Art, Washington D.C.

pastExhibitions.title: All the World's Futures — 56th Venice Biennale

series.name: Bottle-Top Hangings
series.description: Monumental shimmering 'cloths' made from flattened liquor-bottle caps stitched with copper wire — metal that flows like fabric.

series.name: Wooden Wall Sculptures
series.description: Chainsaw-carved and scorched wooden panels exploring Adinkra symbols, language, and rupture.

videos.title: El Anatsui — “When you unite things, their power keeps growing”

name: Julie Mehretu
artStatement: My paintings are palimpsests — layered maps of architecture, migration, protest, and weather. I build dense fields of marks, gestures, and erased photographic source images so that history accumulates and dissolves at once. I am interested in the social agency of abstraction: how a non-figurative surface can hold the pressure of contemporary events without illustrating them.

studio.visits: Private studio; not open to the public.

headshots.credit: Photo: Galerie Magazine — CC BY 3.0 via Wikimedia Commons

education: MFA, Rhode Island School of Design (1997)
awards: U.S. Department of State Medal of Arts (2015)

interviews.title: Julie Mehretu Interview: The In-Between Place
“It's very hard to understand what our reality is.”

publicCollections: The Museum of Modern Art, New York
Whitney Museum of American Art, New York
San Francisco Museum of Modern Art

series.name: Stadia
series.description: Explosive arena-like compositions of flags, banners, and architectural fragments evoking spectacle and global crowds.

series.name: Grey Area
series.description: Quieter, smoke-like canvases where marks are sanded and erased — abstraction approaching atmosphere and loss.

series.name: Recent Paintings
series.description: Large works built over blurred photographic images of protest and conflict, layered with airbrushed colour and gesture.

name: Anish Kapoor
artStatement: Kapoor's sculpture works between the monumental and the void — polished mirror-surfaces that dissolve the object into its surroundings, deep saturated pigment, and engineered voids that pull the eye into apparent bottomlessness. He is preoccupied with the negative, the immaterial and the sublime: the point at which a physical object becomes a perceptual and almost spiritual event.

headshots.credit: Anish Kapoor, 2017 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

awards: Knighthood for services to the visual arts (2013)

series.name: Selected Works
series.description: Public sculpture, mirror works and pigment pieces.

name: Olafur Eliasson
artStatement: Eliasson builds installations from elemental materials — light, water, fog, temperature and reflection — that make perception itself the subject of the work. By staging atmospheric phenomena indoors, he turns viewers into active participants who become aware of their own act of seeing, and increasingly extends that attention to climate and ecology.

headshots.credit: Olafur Eliasson, Berlinale 2017 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

education: Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts, Copenhagen (1989–1995)

videos.title: The Weather Project at Tate Modern

name: Jesús Rafael Soto
artStatement: A founder of kinetic and op art, Soto made works in which line, grid and suspended elements vibrate as the spectator moves, dissolving the boundary between object and space. His immersive 'Penetrables' invited viewers to walk into fields of hanging tubes, replacing the static picture with pure relational movement.

headshots.credit: Jesús Rafael Soto, photo by Lothar Wolleh — Image via Wikimedia Commons

awards: National Prize for Plastic Arts, Venezuela (1960)

name: Carlos Cruz-Diez
artStatement: Cruz-Diez treated colour as an autonomous, time-based event rather than a property of form. Across his Physichromie, Chromosaturation and Chromointerference series he engineered situations in which colour is generated in the eye of the moving viewer, making perception unstable, additive and continuously in flux.

headshots.credit: Carlos Cruz-Diez, France 2013 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

series.name: Selected Works
series.description: Series investigating colour as a perceptual event.

name: James Turrell
artStatement: A central figure of the Light and Space movement, Turrell treats light as a physical, almost tangible material. His Skyspaces, Ganzfelds and the decades-long Roden Crater frame and shape light and sky so that perception turns inward — 'I make spaces that apprehend light for our perception,' he has said, 'and in some ways gather it, or seem to hold it.'

headshots.credit: James Turrell — Image via Wikimedia Commons

series.name: Selected Works
series.description: Light works, Skyspaces and the Roden Crater project.

videos.title: Aten Reign at the Guggenheim & the Skyspaces

name: Ann Veronica Janssens
artStatement: Janssens works with light, colour, mist and reflection to produce ephemeral, destabilising experiences. Her fog-filled rooms saturate space with coloured artificial haze until depth and edges dissolve, foregrounding the fragility and physicality of perception itself.

headshots.credit: Studio portrait

videos.title: Ann Veronica Janssens: Passion for Light

name: Tomás Saraceno
artStatement: Trained as an architect, Saraceno makes floating sculptures, vast cable webs and air-fuelled balloons that imagine new, fossil-free ways of inhabiting the planet. Drawing on spider silk, thermodynamics and the atmosphere, his Aerocene and Cloud Cities projects propose a speculative, ecological reorganisation of how we live and move.

headshots.credit: Tomás Saraceno, Florence 2020 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Conrad Shawcross
artStatement: Shawcross builds mechanical and geometric sculptures rooted in scientific and philosophical ideas — light, motion, harmonics and the limits of knowledge. His kinetic machines and perforated structures translate abstract systems into physical objects that perform, cast shifting shadows, or reach toward the impossible.

headshots.credit: Conrad Shawcross, 2011 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

series.name: Selected Works
series.description: Mechanical sculpture and architectural light works.

works.title: The Dappled Light of the Sun

videos.title: Timepiece in action at the Roundhouse

name: Alicja Kwade
artStatement: Kwade's sculptures and installations probe the subjectivity of time, space and value, using mirrors, stone, metal and everyday objects to stage quiet perceptual paradoxes. Reflections and rotations multiply and displace objects until the viewer can no longer trust what is real, original or merely its double.

headshots.credit: Alicja Kwade, Berlin 2014, photo by Oliver Mark — Image via Wikimedia Commons

series.name: Selected Works
series.description: Installations on time, space and perception.

name: Tomás Sánchez
artStatement: Sánchez paints meticulous, meditative landscapes — vast skies, still waters, dense forests and a solitary figure absorbed in contemplation — informed by his long practice of meditation. A counter-series of monumental landfills (basureros) sets that serenity against images of human waste and ecological reckoning.

headshots.credit: Landscape painting by Tomás Sánchez — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Bridget Riley
artStatement: A central figure of Op art, Riley makes precise abstract paintings of lines, curves and colour that set the eye in motion — producing shimmer, after-images and a sense of instability that locates the artwork in the act of looking itself.

headshots.credit: Portrait

videos.title: Bridget Riley’s Vision: Colour, Line and Perception

name: Felipe Pantone
artStatement: Pantone fuses graffiti, kinetic art and digital aesthetics into hard-edged works of gradient colour, glitch and optical pattern — a visual language for the speed, screens and dynamism of the hyper-digital age.

headshots.credit: Felipe Pantone — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: A Kinetic Wall Sculpture by Felipe Pantone

name: Tauba Auerbach
artStatement: Auerbach works across painting, sculpture, weaving and artists' books to explore structure, dimension and perception — fold paintings, woven canvases and pattern systems that probe how order, space and logic are constructed.

headshots.credit: Portrait

videos.title: Tauba Auerbach in “Bodies of Knowledge” — Art21

name: Sarah Morris
artStatement: Morris makes glossy geometric paintings and films that map the psychology of cities, institutions and power — bright grids and abstracted architecture mirrored by documentary-essay films on urban systems.

headshots.credit: Painting by Sarah Morris — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Julio Le Parc
artStatement: A founder of kinetic and op art and of the Groupe de Recherche d'Art Visuel (GRAV), Le Parc made works of moving light, reflection and instability designed to activate the spectator and dissolve the authority of the static art object.

headshots.credit: Julio Le Parc — Image via Wikimedia Commons

awards: Grand Prize for Painting, Venice Biennale (1966)

videos.title: Julio Le Parc at the Serpentine Sackler Gallery

name: Jim Lambie
artStatement: Lambie turns everyday objects and pop culture into vivid sculptural installations, most famously his 'Zobop' floors of coloured vinyl tape that warp and energise architectural space.

headshots.credit: Jim Lambie — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Spencer Finch
artStatement: Finch records the colour, light and atmosphere of specific places and moments, translating fugitive perceptual experience into installations of filtered light, glass and fluorescent tubes.

headshots.credit: Work by Spencer Finch — Image via Wikimedia Commons

works.title: Trying to Remember the Color of the Sky on That September Morning

videos.title: Tour of Spencer Finch’s Back to Kansas

name: Anthony McCall
artStatement: McCall pioneered 'solid light' works in which a film projector throws slowly evolving lines of light through haze, sculpting volumetric forms that visitors can walk into — film, sculpture, drawing and time collapsed into a single beam.

headshots.credit: Anthony McCall — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Doug Wheeler
artStatement: A founder of the Light and Space movement, Wheeler builds 'infinity environments' — seamless, evenly lit rooms that erase edges and horizon so that light, space and silence become the entire content of the work.

headshots.credit: Portrait

name: Do Ho Suh
artStatement: Suh recreates the architecture of homes he has lived in as translucent, full-scale fabric sculptures — measuring memory, migration and the idea of home as a portable, ghostly space stitched in colored gossamer.

headshots.credit: Do Ho Suh — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Rachel Whiteread
artStatement: Whiteread casts the negative space in and around everyday things — the air inside a room, beneath a chair, within a water tower — turning absence, memory and the overlooked into solid, monumental form.

headshots.credit: Rachel Whiteread, 2018 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

awards: CBE / DBE for services to art

name: Olga de Amaral
artStatement: De Amaral expands weaving into architecture and landscape, building large-scale fiber works layered with gesso, gold and silver leaf that hang in space and shimmer between textile, painting and pre-Columbian heritage.

headshots.credit: Olga de Amaral — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Olga de Amaral, Colombian Textile Artist

name: Lee Ufan
artStatement: A leading figure of Japan's Mono-ha movement, Lee makes spare paintings and sculptures that stage relationships between natural stone, industrial steel and empty space — privileging encounter, process and the unmade over representation.

headshots.credit: Lee Ufan — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Lee Ufan: Why he works with stone and steel

name: Tatsuo Miyajima
artStatement: Miyajima fills space with grids of digital LED counters cycling 1 to 9 — never reaching zero — drawing on Buddhist thought to make installations about time, life, death and continuous change.

headshots.credit: Tatsuo Miyajima, Life (Rhizome) — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Chiharu Shiota
artStatement: Shiota weaves vast webs of red or black thread through entire rooms, entangling keys, beds, boats and dresses — externalizing memory, anxiety, connection and absence as immersive, body-scale drawings in space.

headshots.credit: Chiharu Shiota, The Soul Trembles — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Chiharu Shiota: The Soul Trembles, Paris

name: Teresita Fernández
artStatement: Fernández makes immersive public sculptures and installations that rethink landscape — using materials like graphite, glazed tile, gold and glass to evoke fire, water, cosmos and the politics of how land is seen.

headshots.credit: Teresita Fernández, photo by Natalia Mantini — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Iván Navarro
artStatement: Navarro builds sculptures from fluorescent and neon light, mirrors and glowing tubes that open illusory infinite tunnels — works charged with memory of dictatorship, power, surveillance and the politics of electricity.

headshots.credit: Iván Navarro, Die Again — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Iván Navarro: This Land Is Your Land

name: Ryoji Ikeda
artStatement: Ikeda composes with raw sound, data and light — sine tones, noise and torrents of numbers rendered as vast monochrome projections and beams — making the scale and abstraction of information into a visceral, immersive experience.

headshots.credit: Ryoji Ikeda, Spectra — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Mariko Mori
artStatement: Mori fuses technology, Buddhism and futurism in photographs, video and luminous sculptures — serene, otherworldly environments that imagine a connection between cosmos, nature, ritual and a hybrid future self.

headshots.credit: Mariko Mori — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Mariko Mori: Between the Ancient Gods and the Future

name: Gerhard Richter
artStatement: One of the most important living painters, Richter moves between photo-based realism and pure abstraction — blurred photo-paintings and squeegee-dragged abstractions that interrogate how images, memory and truth are made and doubted.

headshots.credit: Gerhard Richter, Prague 2017 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Gerhard Richter Painting: the master at work

name: Tomma Abts
artStatement: Abts paints small, identically-sized abstract canvases built slowly, without reference, layer upon layer — taut geometries, shadows and reversals that seem to generate their own internal logic and light.

headshots.credit: Portrait

works.title: Untitled abstractions
works.medium: Acrylic and oil on canvas (48 × 38 cm)

name: Mark Bradford
artStatement: Bradford builds large abstract 'paintings' from layered and sanded paper, posters and signage scavenged from the city — maps of social, racial and economic life in which abstraction carries documentary and political weight.

headshots.credit: Mark Bradford — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Mark Grotjahn
artStatement: Grotjahn is known for his 'Butterfly' perspective paintings and dense, carved impasto 'Face' works — abstractions that play one-point perspective and gestural mark-making against each other with intense colour.

headshots.credit: Mark Grotjahn — Image via Wikimedia Commons

works.title: Face series
works.medium: Oil on cardboard mounted on linen

name: Tomory Dodge
artStatement: Dodge paints thickly worked canvases that hover between abstraction and landscape — fragmented, gestural fields of strokes and debris that coalesce into image and then dissolve back into paint.

headshots.credit: Portrait

name: Tom McGlynn
artStatement: McGlynn is an American artist, writer and educator whose hard-edged, reductive abstract paintings and works on paper draw on signage and vernacular geometry, pursuing what he calls a 'non-relational' visual order. (He is also an art critic and curator.)

headshots.credit: Portrait

videos.title: In the Studio with Tom McGlynn

name: Sarah Cain
artStatement: Cain makes exuberant, improvisational paintings that spill off the canvas onto floors, walls, windows and found objects — site-responsive installations that treat painting as a physical, immersive and joyful act.

headshots.credit: Sarah Cain, installation at SFO — Image via Wikimedia Commons

works.title: We Will Walk Right Up To the Sun
Now I'm going to tell you everything

name: Katharina Grosse
artStatement: Grosse uses an industrial spray gun to flood architecture, landscape and objects with sweeping fields of saturated colour — liberating painting from the canvas into immersive, site-swallowing environments.

headshots.credit: Katharina Grosse, Berlin 2011, photo by Oliver Mark — Image via Wikimedia Commons

works.title: The Horse Trotted Another Couple of Metres, Then It Stopped

videos.title: Katharina Grosse: Painting with Color — Art21

name: Tom Friedman
artStatement: Friedman is a conceptual sculptor who transforms humble materials — paper, foil, sugar cubes, styrofoam, his own materials pushed to obsessive extremes — into witty, precise objects that test perception, scale and the limits of making.

headshots.credit: Tom Friedman — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: In the Studio with Tom Friedman

name: Doris Salcedo
artStatement: Salcedo makes sculpture and public interventions that mourn the victims of political violence — furniture filled with concrete, a fissure cut into a museum floor — turning grief, absence and testimony into materially exacting form.

headshots.credit: Doris Salcedo — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Haroon Mirza
artStatement: Mirza orchestrates electricity, LEDs, sound and found media into sculptural installations where current itself is the medium — composing rhythmic circuits that blur the line between noise, music, light and sculpture.

headshots.credit: Portrait

works.title: The National Apavilion of Then and Now

videos.title: Lisson Gallery talks to Haroon Mirza

name: Rafael Lozano-Hemmer
artStatement: Lozano-Hemmer makes large-scale interactive installations using robotics, sensors, light and biometric data — public works completed only by participation, recording heartbeats, breath and presence as collective portraits.

headshots.credit: Rafael Lozano-Hemmer — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Nairy Baghramian
artStatement: Baghramian makes elegant, bodily abstract sculptures in cast metal, glass, silicone and wax that lean, prop and rest against architecture — probing the body, support, fragility and the relationship of object to space.

headshots.credit: Nairy Baghramian, Beliebte Stellen — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Carsten Höller
artStatement: Trained as a scientist, Höller designs experiential installations — giant slides, rotating beds, mirror carousels and perception-altering devices — that treat the visitor as a test subject and doubt as a creative method.

headshots.credit: Carsten Höller, slide at LUMA Arles — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Carsten Höller: Test Site, Tate Modern (2006)

name: Monika Sosnowska
artStatement: Sosnowska makes large steel sculptures that twist, crush and contort the modernist architecture of post-war Poland — staircases, gates and facades wrenched into knots that register utopian ambition and its collapse.

headshots.credit: Monika Sosnowska, Stairs — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Josiah McElheny
artStatement: McElheny is a sculptor working primarily in hand-blown glass and mirror, building dazzling reflective assemblages and cosmological models that fuse modernist design, history and ideas about infinity and light.

headshots.credit: Portrait

name: Tony Oursler
artStatement: A pioneer of video sculpture, Oursler projects talking faces and eyes onto dolls, eggs and sculptural forms — uncanny, muttering effigies that explore media, identity, psychology and our entanglement with screens.

headshots.credit: Tony Oursler — Image via Wikimedia Commons

name: Refik Anadol
artStatement: Anadol is a pioneer of data-driven art, using machine-learning models trained on vast datasets to generate immersive 'data sculptures' and 'machine hallucinations' — fluid, dreaming projections that visualize memory, nature and the mind of the machine.

headshots.credit: Refik Anadol — Image via Wikimedia Commons

works.title: Unsupervised
works.status: Museum of Modern Art, New York
